title: Losing the silly is serious
html_body:
Today in Science and science: Asexual fish, talkative seals, and more
Brought to you by From Concept to Reality: Taking Intelligent Agents into the Real World
View this email in your browser
13 March 2026
In today’s Logbook, freelance reporter Hannah Richter discusses a sobering change to science’s silliest awards ceremony. But first, catch up on the latest science news, including the genetic quirks of an all-female fish species and magnetic engines that may power the universe’s brightest stellar explosions.
evolution  |  News from Science
A genetic trick helps asexual fish beat the odds
Once upon a time, a female Atlantic molly (Poecilia mexicana) living in the fresh waters near what is now Tampico, Mexico, mated with a male sailfin molly (Poecilia latipinna). The offspring of this cross-species coupling ought to have been sterile, like a mule. But this particular hybrid went on to birth a brood of daughters—all of which were genetic clones of their mother.

Scientists have long assumed that birthing clones would be a ticking time bomb for vertebrate animals, with offspring inevitably succumbing to genomic degradation over time. But the Amazon molly (Poecilia formosa), named for the fierce female warriors of Greek mythology, has survived and thrived for at least 100,000 years—10 times longer than predicted by evolutionary theory. According to a study published this week in Nature, it all comes down to a DNA repair process known as gene conversion , in which one version of a gene overwrites the other. Although not nearly as effective as sexual recombination, this process allows the fish to purge some harmful mutations; it creates genetic diversity for natural selection to act on, helping stave off evolutionary doom.

“We always thought it was an evolutionary dead end, if you’re asexual,” says parasitologist Annette MacLeod, who wasn’t involved in the new study. “But it doesn’t have to be.”
Read the full story
cognitive neuroscience  |  Science
Seals have complex vocal learning on the brain
This harbor seal is more than just a cute face—it also has an impressive capacity for flexible vocal learning.  marcel burkhard
In the late 1970s and early 1980s, a harbor seal named Hoover delighted visitors to the New England Aquarium with his uncanny ability to mimic human speech, barking out phrases like “Hello, there!” and “Come over here!” in a thick Maine accent. In general, pinnipeds—the group of marine mammals that includes seals and sea lions—are capable of complex vocal learning and flexibility.

To investigate the neurobiological basis of these abilities, scientists examined postmortem brains from harbor seals, elephant seals, and California sea lions . They also looked at brains from coyotes, which howl in chorus with one another but don’t demonstrate flexible vocal learning, as a comparison. The team found that seals and sea lions, but not coyotes, showed a strong, direct connection between the vocal motor cortex and a part of the brainstem involved in speech—a neural pathway associated with voluntary control of vocalization. Elephant and harbor seals also possessed a forebrain circuit similar to the one involved in vocal learning and mimicry in birds.

These differences in neural connectivity, the researchers reported, could explain why some pinniped species seem to have a greater capacity for vocal flexibility and learning. Hoover, of course, remains in a class of his own.
Read the Paper
space  |  News from Science
Supercharged supernova bumps into explanation
In 2003, astronomers witnessed the blast of a dying star that was brighter than anything seen before. Scientists have since detected several hundred of these “superluminous” supernovae, but their brightness remains a mystery. A typical supernova is a dying star that blasts out a lot of shrapnel that glows brightly for weeks, briefly outshining its home galaxy. But glowing debris alone can’t explain superluminous supernovae, which are 10 or more times brighter.

Now, a new superluminous supernova with strange, periodic bumps in its emission is shedding some light on the puzzle. The pattern of bumps, according to a report in Nature this week, could only be produced by a powerful magnetic engine made from the remnants of the exploded star, and which turbocharges its expanding debris from within.

While many superluminous supernovae show bumps in their brightness, astronomers found that the bumps for this particular supernova followed a steady pattern and their rate and peak height were reducing. Such regularity couldn’t come from random processes in an expanding ball of debris, they argue. Instead, the team concluded that the core of the progenitor star collapsed into a superdense highly magnetized ball of matter called a magnetar. If the magnetar is ringed by a disk made of leftover star-stuff, it may wobble like a top, explaining the periodic brightness bumps.

It’s hard to prove such a model with a single example, but this bumpy and bright supernova has got astrophysicists thinking. “There’s not many other things you can think of that can power something so bright,” said astrophysicist Daniel Kasen, who was not involved in the work.
Read the full story

Wallonia: A story of biopharma growth
What does biopharma growth look like on the ground in Wallonia, Belgium? This Q&A explores how the region has built research capacity, expanded lab space, and supported companies through public investment, clinical networks, and talent development.
Read more
Logbook
At the 2024 Ig Nobel Prize ceremony, researchers who used chromatography to separate drunk and sober worms snagged the chemistry prize.  steven senne/ap
Losing the silly is serious
Hannah Richter, freelance reporter
In 2024, I had the good fortune of traveling to Cambridge, Massachusetts to report on the Ig Nobels for ScienceAdviser. The ceremony was lovably bizarre, featuring costumes, props, paper airplanes, and researchers sheepishly excited to be honored for their experiments.

Then, in 2025, my reporting took a turn. The Ig Nobels were still wonderfully wacky, but with a somber undertone: Nearly half of the winning groups were absent . I wrote of how wars, visa restrictions, and President Donald Trump’s research and border policies had affected scientists’ desire to travel to the United States. Even schmoozing with true Nobel Laureates wasn’t compelling enough to override fears of having phones searched or not returning home on time.

In the weeks following the ceremony, Ig Nobels founder and master of ceremonies, Marc Abrahams, reflected on the winners’ absences. He was “aware that we might have a similar situation this year,” he said. But even since the most recent awards last September, “the situation got far worse.” The U.S. has launched strikes on Iran, and international travelers continue to be stopped for questioning or even turned away at the U.S. border.

This week, I covered the Ig Nobels’ announcement that they will be moving to Europe after 35 years in the Boston area. In a press release, Abrahams wrote that the Ig Nobels could no longer, “in good conscience,” ask international winners or journalists to travel to the U.S.

Luca Luiselli, a 2025 Ig Nobel winner for his research demonstrating that African rainbow lizards prefer four cheese pizza, “totally agreed” with the decision. He cited his own choice not to attend the 2025 ceremony due to a fear of crossing the U.S. border with a social media presence that criticizes Trump. “It is simply too dangerous for a European to enter the USA,” he said.

The international move isn’t solely about politics. The new host institutions in Zurich, Switzerland offered institutional funding, which the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Harvard University, and Boston University—all of which have previously hosted the ceremony—never provided. But it’s hard to ignore the metaphor of U.S. scientists and science-lovers losing easy access to science’s most lighthearted affair.

Abrahams told me he expects the Ig Nobels to return to American soil “at some point.” Until then, I hear Zurich is lovely in fall.
Read the Full Story
podcast
What Alaska’s eroding coastline says about Earth’s future, and how Yellowstone ravens use their smarts to find wolf kills
By SARAH CRESPI, evan howell   |   12 March 2026
Et Cetera
A bone to pick
Analysis of a massive fossil leg bone may suggest that the ancestors of Tyrannosaurus rex came from North America, but some paleontologists remain unconvinced.
Scientific Reports Paper  |  Read more at Science News
Hope for hogs
New research has revealed that hedgehogs can hear very high frequencies, which means scientists could potentially use ultrasonic signals to keep the animals away from dangerous roads.
Biology Letters Paper  |  Read more at The Guardian
For all intents and porpoises
In 2021 and 2023, hundreds of dolphins became stranded on the shores of Patagonia. Now, scientists think they have identified the culprit: orca whales.
Royal Society Open Science Paper  |  Read more at Scientific American
Last but not least
King penguins are one of the few species that actually seem to be thriving in response to climate change. Unfortunately, scientists aren’t sure how long that success can last.
Phie Jacobs, General Assignment Reporter, Science

With contributions from Daniel Clery and Hannah Richter

items.title: it all comes down to a DNA repair process known as gene conversion
uncanny ability to mimic human speech
could only be produced by a powerful magnetic engine made from the remnants of the exploded star
report on the Ig Nobels for Science Adviser
actually seem to be thriving in response to climate change
